## **›r∂madbhagavad-G∂tå** 

## **Chapter I** 

The present chapter is the Introduction to the great teachings delivered to the entire world by God Himself in the form of the _G∂tµa,_ making Arjuna the immediate cause for its delivery. After a recital of the principal warriors on both sides, it mainly Title of the describes Arjunaís dejection, caused by faint-heartedness, from the fear of loss Chapter of friends and relatives in the course of the impending clash of arms. And such a feeling of dejection helps oneís spiritual advancement by inducing aversion to worldly enjoyments if one comes in touch with an advanced Soul. That is why the chapter has been given the title of ëëThe Yoga of Dejection of Arjunaíí. 

In the first verse of this chapter Dhætarµa¶¢ra enquires of Sa¤jaya about the details of the war. Thereupon Sa¤jaya describes in the second verse how, approaching Droƒµacµarya, Duryodhana starts his conversation. In the third verse Duryodhana invites Summary of Droƒµacµarya to observe the mighty army of the PµaƒŒavas and devotes verses the Chapter 4 to 6 to a recital of the names of the prominent warriors on the PµaƒŒava side. In the seventh, asking Droƒµacµarya to know pointedly the main warriors and generals of his own army, he gives in verses 8 and 9 the names of some of them and describes their heroism and skill in warfare. In the tenth verse declaring his own army as unconquerable, and that of the PµaƒŒavas as comparatively weaker, in the eleventh he requests all his warriors to guard Bh∂¶ma on all sides. The twelfth verse speaks of Bh∂¶ma blowing his conch and the thirteenth describes the noise produced by the sudden blaring forth of conches, kettledrums, drums and trumpets, etc., in the Kaurava army. Fourteenth to eighteenth verses speak of Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa, Arjuna, Bh∂ma, Yudhi¶¢hira, Nakula, Sahadeva and all other distinguished warriors of the PµaƒŒava army blowing their respective conches and verse 19 tells us of the terrible sound echoing through heaven and earth and rending the heart of Duryodhana and his followers. Seeing the sons of Dhætarµa¶¢ra arrayed for battle Arjuna requests ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa, in verses 20 and 21, to place the chariot between the two armies, and in verses 22 and 23 he says that the chariot should be kept there till he has carefully observed and scanned the warriors assembled for the war. Verses 24 and 25 describe how placing the chariot between the two armies, as desired by Arjuna, ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa invites the latter to behold the warriors assembled for the war. Then, upto verse 30 there is a description, first by Sa¤jaya and then by Arjuna himself, of the latterís perplexity and grief at the sight of his relations in battle-array. In verse 31 Arjuna points out the evil consequences of war and verses 32 and 33 are devoted to his reasons for not coveting either victory or the pleasures attending sovereignty. In verses 34 and 35 Arjuna mentions his close relationship with the warriors, viz., they being his teachers, uncles, etc., and declares that he did not want to kill them, though he might be killed by them, or even for the sovereignty of the three worlds. Saying so, he proceeds in verses 36 and 

***[Bhagavad-G∂tµa ] *** 

**32** 

37 to say that even though Duryodhana and his brothers were desperadoes, their killing would result only in sin and happiness could never be expected from it. In verses 38 and 39 he points out why the PµaƒŒavas should desist from the sin of destruction of their own race and enmity towards friends, and devotes verses 40 to 44 to a detailed enumeration of the evils resulting from the destruction of a family and its traditions. In verses 45 and 46 Arjuna says that the preparation for war with a view to killing his own relations due to lust for throne and enjoyment was nothing but preparation for the commission of a great sin, and expressing regret for it, he declared that it was better that the sons of Dhætarµa¶¢ra should kill him. The chapter is concluded with verse 47, wherein Sa¤jaya describes how having determined not to fight, and agitated by grief, Arjuna laid down his arms, and sank into his chariot. 

_The pomp and wealth of the PµaƒŒavas displayed during the performance of the Rµajasµuya sacrifice aroused deep jealousy in the mind of Duryodhana, who in collusion with ›akuni, etc., invited Yudhi¶¢hira to a game of dice, in which Yudhi¶¢hira Link of the was fraudulently defeated and deprived of all his wealth and possessions. Story Finally, it was settled that Yudhi¶¢hira and the other PµaƒŒavas, together with Draupad∂, should repair to the forest and live there in exile for twelve years; after this for one year they were to remain incognito, untraced by the Kauravas. All these thirteen years the kingdom was to be ruled by Duryodhana, and if the PµaƒŒavas remained undetected during the stipulated period of one yearís incognito existence, the kingdom was to be returned to them after the conclusion of thirteen years. Having successfully concluded their exile of thirteen years according to these terms when the PµaƒŒavas at last asked for the return of their kingdom, Duryodhana gave a flat refusal. The learned and aged family priest of Drupada was sent to Duryodhanaís court to negotiate in favour of the PµaƒŒavas, but Duryodhana remained adamant. Thereafter both the sides began to prepare themselves for war. Duryodhana went to Dvµarakµa to invite Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa to join him as an ally. Arjuna also reached there the very same day. They both found ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa resting on a couch in His palace. Observing that ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa was asleep, Duryodhana went in and occupied a nice and comfortable seat placed at the head of the couch, while Arjuna remained standing near the feet of ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa, joining both his palms in a posture of humility. The moment ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa opened His eyes He saw Arjuna standing before Him, and then, when He turned His head, He could observe Duryodhana seated on the chair at the head of the couch. Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa welcomed both of them, and asked for the reasons of their visit. In reply Duryodhana said, ëëYour love and affection for me as well as Arjuna are quite balanced, and both of us are your relations; but I was the first to approach you. The code of honour among honourable men demands that help should be rendered to the first seeker of help. You are the greatest and most honourable person in the world today; therefore, you should render help to me alone.íí The Lord said, ëëI agree that you were the first to come here; but My eyes fell on Arjuna first. Therefore, I shall help both of you. According to the law of the scripture, the privilege of first choice should be given to the younger in age, therefore Arjunaís desire should be satisfied first. My help will be available in this war in two ways. One side will have_ 

***[Chapter I ] *** 

**33** 

_My most powerful Nµarµayaƒ∂-Senµa, and on the other, I shall remain Myself, single-handed, bound by the vow of not participating in battle, and not taking up arms. Now, Arjuna, as righteousness dictates, I give you the first chance to express your desire; please take from out of those two whatever you preferíí. Arjuna thereupon chose Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa, the slayer of foes, and a manifestation of Nµarµayaƒa Himself, as his helper. Duryodhana, for his part, took the powerful Nµarµayaƒ∂-Senµa as his share, and returned to Hastinµapura very much delighted at heart._ 

_Then, Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa asked Arjuna why, when He (›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa) would not participate in battle as a warrior, Arjuna gave up preference for the Nµarµayaƒ∂-Senµa and took Him (›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa) on his side Arjuna replied, ëëLord ! You are capable of destroying all those forces single-handed; why should I, in that case, care for the army ? Besides, I have for a long time cherished the desire in my heart that you should act as my Charioteer. Kindly fulfil that desire of mine during this great waríí. The Lord, who is ever the most devoted lover of His devotees, accepted with pleasure this role of driving the horses of Arjunaís chariot, as desired by Arjuna. That is how Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa became the charioteer of Arjuna, and at the commencement of the battle of Kuruk¶etra delivered to Arjuna the divine teachings as incorporated in the G∂tµa._ 

_On the return of Duryodhana and Arjuna from Dvµarakµa, when the armies on both sides had assembled, Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa Himself went to Hastinµapura as the emissarry of the PµaƒŒavas, and tried to prevail upon Duryodhana to prevent the war; but Duryodhana declared in clear and explicit terms : ëëSo long as I am alive, the PµaƒŒavas can never expect to have the kingdom. I am not prepared to give them even as much land as can be covered by the point of a needle.íí (Mahµabhµarata, Udyoga-Parva, Chap. 127, verses 22 to 25). It is only then that according to the advice of their mother, Kunt∂, and under the inspiration of Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa, the PµaƒŒavas finally decided to resort to war, considering their cause to be righteous, and establish by force of arms their rightful claim to the kingdom._ 

_When both the sides had thoroughly prepared to start the battle, the sage Vedavyµasa approached Dhætarµa¶¢ra and said, ëëIf you want to see this terrible carnage with your own eyes, I can make you a gift of transcendent vision.íí But Dhætarµa¶¢ra replied: ëëO chief of Brahmar¶is, I have no desire to see with my own eyes this slaughter of my own family, but I should like to hear all the details of the battleíí. Thereupon the great sage Vedavyµasa conferred the gift of divine vision on Sa¤jaya (Dhætarµa¶¢raís trusty counsellor) and said to Dhætarµa¶¢ra, ìSa¤jaya will describe to you all the incidents of the warî. Whatever happens in the course of this war, he will directly see, hear and otherwise come to know. Whether an incident takes place before his eyes or behind his back, during the daytime or at night, privately or in public, and whether it is reduced to actual action or appears only as a thought, it will not remain hidden from his view. He will come to know everything exactly as it happens. No weapon will touch his body, nor will he feel the least exhaustion._ 

_ëëWhat is taking place is inevitable; there is no power on earth capable of arresting the course of this catastrophe. It will end in the triumph of righteousness and virtue.íí_ 

***[Bhagavad-G∂tµa ] *** 

**34** 

_On Mahar¶i Vedavyµasaís departure from the royal court, Sa¤jaya in answer to Dhætarµa¶¢raís query described to him the location of the different Dv∂pas (divisions) of the terrestrial world in the course of which he gave a geographical description of Bhµaratavar¶a (India) as well. In the meanwhile, the struggle between the Kauravas and PµaƒŒavas started and continued for ten days, when the great Bh∂¶ma was thrown down from his chariot and lay on his bed of arrows. At this point Sa¤jaya, again, goes to Dhætarµa¶¢ra and suddenly announces to him the fall of Bh∂¶ma in battle (Bh∂¶ma-Parva, Chap. 13). Terribly moved at the sad news, Dhætarµa¶¢ra requested Sa¤jaya to describe to him all the incidents of the conflict. Thereupon Sa¤jaya gave a detailed description of the formation of the two rival armies. Then, Dhætarµa¶¢ra again asked Sa¤jaya to recite to him in greater detail all the incidents that had taken place from the beginning of the battle till then. Here commences the first chapter of the G∂tµa. It constitutes the twentyfifth chapter of Bh∂¶ma-Parva of the Mahµabhµarata. It starts with Dhætarµa¶¢ra questioning Sa¤jaya in the following terms:_ 

_äÊÎÃ⁄UÊc≈˛U ©UflÊø_ **œ◊¸ˇÊòÊ ∑È§L§ˇÊòÊ ‚◊flÃÊ ÿÈÿÈà‚fl—– ◊Ê◊∑§Ê— ¬Êá«UflÊpÒfl Á∑§◊∑È§fl¸Ã ‚TÿH 1H** 

**œÎÃ⁄UÊc≈˛U—** Dhætarµa¶¢ra, the blind king of Hastinµapura; **©UflÊø** said; **œ◊¸ˇÊòÊ ∑È§L§ˇÊòÊ** on the sacred soil of Kuruk¶etra; **‚◊flÃÊ—** gathered; **ÿÈÿÈà‚fl—** eager to fight; **◊Ê◊∑§Ê—** my own sons; **¬Êá«UflÊ—** the sons of PµaƒŒu; **ø** and; **∞fl** indeed; **Á∑§◊˜** what; **•∑È§fl¸Ã** did; **‚Tÿ** O Sa¤jaya. **Dhætarµa¶¢ra said: Sa¤jaya, gathered on the holy land of Kuruk¶etra, eager to fight, what did my sons and the sons of PµaƒŒu do? (1)** 

Chapter 83 of _Vanaparva_ , and Chapter 53 of _›alyaparva_ , of the _Mahµabhµarata_ , contain detailed disquisitions on the sacredness of Kuruk¶etra. It has been described as bounded by the river Sarasvat∂ in the north, and the river Dæ¶advat∂ in the south. It is said that the length and breadth of the area were five Yojanas each, corresponding to forty miles on each side. The present geographical position of the place is south of Ambala in Haryana and north of Delhi. A small town, named Kuruk¶etra, is even now situated at the place. There is another name, Samantapa¤caka, by which the locality is designated. The _›atapatha-Brµahmaƒa_ and other sacred books mention it as the place where celestials like Agni, Indra and Brahmµa performed their austerities. King Kuru also performed severe 

austerities at this very place. Those who die here go to the higher regions after death. For these and similar other reasons ëKuruk¶etraí is called a Dharma-K¶etra or Puƒya-K¶etra, ëholy tractí. By the word ëMµamakµa¨, (my sons) Dhætarµa¶¢ra refers to his hundred sons and all the warriors on his side. And by the word ëPµaƒŒavµa¨í (the sons of PµaƒŒu), he means the five PµaƒŒava brothers, Yudhi¶¢hira and others, and all the warriors on their side. The questioning in the verse shows Dhætarµa¶¢raís desire to know in detail the account of the terrible fight which had raged for ten days prior to this,óhow the assembled fighters started the battle, who was matched with whom in the fighting array, and who was killed by whom, on what date and by what means, etc. 

***[Chapter I ] *** 

**35** 

Dhætarµa¶¢ra having already heard the news of the great Bh∂¶maís fall in battle, the questioning cannot mean that he had remained wholly ignorant of the battle, and therefore wanted to know whether the sanctity of the ëholy landí of Kuruk¶etra had reformed the mind of his sons, inducing them to make peace with the PµaƒŒavas by conceding to the 

latter their right to their kingdom, or whether the righteous King Yudhi¶¢hira himself, being influenced by the holiness of the tract, had walked out of the war, or whether both the armies had, till then, remained arrayed in battle in their respective positions, and no fight had yet taken place, or, if there had been a fight, what was its result, etc. 

_In answer to Dhætarµa¶¢raís query, Sa¤jaya says:_ 

## _‚Tÿ ©UflÊø_ **ŒÎ¶ÔÊ ÃÈ ¬Êá«UflÊŸË∑¢§ √ÿÍ…¢U ŒÈÿÊ¸œŸSÃŒÊ– ⁄UÊ¡Ê •ÊøÊÿ¸◊È¬‚XÔUêÿ fløŸ◊’˝flËÃ˜H 2H** 

**‚Tÿ—** Sa¤jaya; **©UflÊø** said: **ŒÎ¶ÔUÊ** seeing; **ÃÈ** and; **¬Êá«UflÊŸË∑§◊˜** the army of the PµaƒŒavas; **√ÿÍ…U◊˜** drawn up for battle; **ŒÈÿÊ¸œŸ—** Duryodhana; **ÃŒÊ** at that time; **•ÊøÊÿ¸◊˜** the preceptor (Droƒa); **©U¬‚XÔUêÿ** approaching; **⁄UÊ¡Ê** king; **fløŸ◊˜** (these) words; **•’˝flËÃ˜** spoke. **Sa¤jaya said: At that time, seeing the army of the PµaƒŒavas drawn up for battle and approaching Droƒµacµarya, King Duryodhana spoke these words:         (2)** 

Sa¤jaya refers to Duryodhana as ëRµajµaí, or King. The following may be the reasons for it: 

(a) Duryodhana was an eminent hero and a great statesman and was actually running the whole Government. 

(b) It is the nature of saints to show respect to all, and Sa¤jaya was a man of saintly character. 

(c) An epithet of respect applied to the son would bring pleasure to Dhætarµa¶¢raís heart. 

The PµaƒŒava army had been arrayed in such a spectacular manner that its very sight startled Duryodhana, and in an anxious mood he hastened himself to inform Droƒµacµarya about it. His idea was that after an examination of the formation of the PµaƒŒava army, the great Teacher of Military Science, Droƒa, would advise Bh∂¶ma, the Chief Commander, to form a battle-array which would be better and more impregnable than that of the PµaƒŒava army. 

Though the grand old man Bh∂¶ma was the Chief Commander of the Kaurava army, the position which Guru Droƒµacµarya, held in that army was also very high and responsible. In an army the position allotted to a commander must be maintained by him; he cannot quit his position without throwing the army into disorder. Therefore, though exercising the authority of the King, Duryodhana considered it better to go to Droƒµacµarya himself, rather than summon the Åcµarya to him and thus make him move from his allotted place in the army. Over and above this, Droƒµacµarya was old in age and advanced in knowledge, and being the Teacher of the Kurus was worthy of respect and honour. Again, Duryodhana had to use the Åcµarya as an instrument for serving his own selfish end; for this as well it was desirable to secure to goodwill of the Åcµarya by proper exhibition of honour. From the spiritual point of view, it is oneís duty to show humility and respect 

***[Bhagavad-G∂tµa ] *** 

**36** 

by oneís conduct towards every other being; respect to others. From all these points of nay, in politics as well, a shrewd and clever view, it was but meet and proper for man in order to get his purpose served shows Duryodhana to go to Droƒµacµarya himself. 

## _Approaching Droƒµacµarya, what Duryodhana said is stated below:_ **¬‡ÿÒÃÊ¢ ¬Êá«ÈU¬ÈòÊÊáÊÊ◊ÊøÊÿ¸ ◊„UÃË¥ ø◊Í◊Ô˜– √ÿÍ…UÊ¢ º˝È¬Œ¬ÈòÊáÊ Ãfl Á‡ÊcÿáÊ œË◊ÃÊH 3H** 

**¬‡ÿ** behold; **∞ÃÊ◊˜Ô** yonder; **¬Êá«ÈU¬ÈòÊÊáÊÊ◊˜Ô** of the sons of PµaƒŒu; **•ÊøÊÿ¸** master; **◊„UÃË◊˜Ô** mighty; **ø◊Í◊˜** army; **√ÿÍ…UÊ◊˜Ô** arrayed for battle; **º˝È¬Œ¬ÈòÊáÊ** Dhæ¶¢adyumna (son of Drupada); **Ãfl Á‡ÊcÿáÊ œË◊ÃÊ** by your talented pupil. 

**Behold, O Revered Master, the mighty army of the sons of PµaƒŒu arrayed for battle by your talented pupil, Dhæ¶¢adyumna, son of Drupada.                       (3)** 

As a diplomat, Duryodhana was exceptionally clever. In order to excite Droƒµacµarya by rousing his revengeful spirit against Dhæ¶¢adyumna and ire against the PµaƒŒavas, he referred to Dhæ¶¢adyumna as Drupadaís son and 'your talented pupil'. By these words he reminded Droƒa of Drupadaís unseemly behaviour towards him in the long past, and then of his getting Dhæ¶¢adyumna as his son by the performance of a sacrifice with the unholy motive of killing Droƒa. By implication, he also said that Dhæ¶¢adyumna was so clever and the Åcµarya so simple that though born to kill him, he had obtained from him teaching in the science and art of archery. And, then, how skilful and dexterous he was to array his army in such an impregnable way as to baffle his opponents ! It was such 

a person whom the PµaƒŒavas had made their Supreme Commander. It was now for the Åcµarya to consider what should be his line of action. 

The Kaurava army consisted of eleven Ak¶auhiƒ∂s,* and the PµaƒŒava army only of seven. Though numerically smaller, the PµaƒŒava army appeared very huge because of its adoption of the military array known as ëVajravyµuhaí. Besides, a smaller army might be more powerful than a larger one if its deployment was better and more efficient than that of the larger army. That is why calling the army of the PµaƒŒavas ëa mighty armyí, Duryodhana attempted to draw the Åcµaryaís attention to it, and appealed to him to devise some means of getting the better of it.

**•òÊ** (There are) in this army; **‡ÊÍ⁄UÊ—Ô** heroes (such as); **◊„UcflÊ‚Ê—** wielding mighty bows; **÷Ë◊Ê¡¸ÈŸ‚◊Ê—Ô** equal to Bh∂ma and Arjuna; **ÿÈÁœ** in military prowess; **ÿÈÿÈœÊŸ—Ô** a name of Sµatyaki; **Áfl⁄UÊ≈U—** Virµa¢a; **ø** and; **º˝È¬Œ—** Drupada; **ø** and; **◊„UÊ⁄UÕ—** the warrior chief; **œÎC∑§ÃÈ—Ô** Dhæ¶¢aketu; **øÁ∑§ÃÊŸ—Ô** Cekitµana; **∑§ÊÁ‡Ê⁄UÊ¡—** the King of Kµa‹∂ (the modern Vµarµaƒas∂); **ø** and; **flËÿ¸flÊŸ˜** valiant; **¬ÈL§Á¡Ã˜** Purujit (lit., the conqueror of many); **∑È§ÁãÃ÷Ê¡—Ô** Kuntibhoja; **øÔ** and; **‡ÊÒéÿ—** ›aibya; **ø** and; **Ÿ⁄U¬ÈXÔUfl—Ô** the best of men; **ÿÈœÊ◊ãÿÈ—** Yudhµamanyu; **ø** and; **Áfl∑˝§ÊãÃ—** mighty; **©UûÊ◊ÊÒ¡Ê—** Uttamaujµa; (lit., one who possesses supreme strength); **øÔ** and; **flËÿ¸flÊŸ˜** valiant; **‚ÊÒ÷º˝—Ô** the son of Subhadrµa, Abhimanyu; **º˝ÊÒ¬ŒÿÊ—** the (five) sons of Draupad∂; **ø** and; **‚fl¸** all (of them); **∞fl** indeed; **◊„UÊ⁄UÕÊ—** warrior chiefs. 

**There are in this army heroes wielding mighty bows and equal in military prowess to Bh∂ma and ArjunaóSµatyaki and Virµa¢a and the Mahµarath∂ (warrior chief) Drupada; Dhæ¶¢aketu, Cekitµana and the valiant King of Kµa‹∂, and Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and ›aibya, the best of men, and mighty Yudhµamanyu, and valiant Uttamaujµa, Abhimanyu, the son of Subhadrµa, and the five sons of Draupad∂,óall of them Mahµarath∂s (warrior chiefs). (4,5,6)** 

The term ëYudhií in verse 4 cannot be interpreted as the substantive of ëAtraí, as some are prone to take it; for the battle had not actually commenced till then. Besides, the PµaƒŒava army having been referred to in the immediately preceding verse, the term ëAtraí naturally refers to it, and no substantive is thus required to go with it. By connecting the word ëYudhií with ëBh∂mµarjunasamµa¨í, it has been indicated that the famous warriors whose names follow equalled Bh∂ma and Arjuna in prowess and proficiency in the art of warfare. 

The history and career of the warriors whose names appear in these verses are given below: 

Yuyudhµana was the other name of Sµatyaki, who was Arjunaís disciple _(vide Mahµabhµarata, Udyoga-Parva,_ Chapter 81, verses 5-8). He was the grandson of ›ini, a Yµadava Chief _(Mahµabhµarata, Droƒa-Parva,_ Chapter 144, verses 17-19). He was very much attached to Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa and was a powerful warrior and an ëAtirath∂í, who could fight any number of warriors single-handed. Having survived the Mahµabhµarata War, he met with his death in 

the internecine feud that followed among the Yµadavas. There was another Yµadava warrior bearing the name of Yuyudhµana, who is mentioned in _Mahµabhµarata Udyoga-Parva,_ Chapter 152, verse 6. 

Virµa¢a was the name of the virtuous king of the Matsyas. The PµaƒŒavas spent their one yearís incognito existence under him. His daughter, Uttarµa, was given in marriage to Arjunaís son, Abhimanyu. Virµa¢a and his three sons, Uttara, ›veta and ›a∆kha, were killed in the Mahµabhµarata war. Drupada was the son of King Pæ¶at of the Pµa¤cµalas. King Pæ¶at and Sage Bharadvµaja were great friends; therefore, Drupada passed a certain period of his early days in the hermitage of Sage Bharadvµaja, where an intimacy was formed between him and Droƒa, the son of Sage Bharadvµaja. On the demise of Pæ¶at, when Drupada had become king of the Pµa¤cµalas, Droƒa went to see him on a certain occasion, and addressed him as a friend. But this was resented by Drupada, and Droƒa came away wounded at heart. Imparting the knowledge of archery both to the Kauravas and the PµaƒŒavas, Droƒa realized 

***[Bhagavad-G∂tµa ] *** 

**38** 

the preceptorís fee by having Drupada vanquished in battle by Arjuna in requital of the insult he had suffered at Drupadaís hands, and appropriated half of the latterís kingdom. Vanquished in battle, Drupada reestablished his friendship with Droƒa only as a matter of show, nursing a grievance against him in the core of his heart. With the help of a couple of Brahmar¶is, named Yµaja and Upayµaja, he performed a sacrifice with the motive of obtaining a son who would kill Droƒa. From the altar of that sacrifice sprang up both Dhæ¶¢adyumna and Kæ¶ƒµa. It was this Kæ¶ƒµa who later on became known in history by the names of Draupad∂ and Yµaj¤asen∂, and whom the PµaƒŒavas married after winning her hand in an open trial of skill in archery. King Drupada was a great K¶atriya hero and was counted as a Mahµarath∂. In the Mahµabhµarata War he was killed by Droƒa in an open fight _(Mahµa., Droƒa.,_ Ch. 136). 

Dhæ¶¢aketu was the son of ›i‹upµala, King of Cedi. He met his death at the hands of Droƒa in the Mahµabhµarata War _(Mahµa., Droƒa.,_ 125). 

Cekitµana was a Yµadava hero belonging to the clan of Væ¶ƒis _(Mahµa., Bh∂¶ma.,_ 84.20), a Mahµarath∂ as a fighter and possessed of great prowess. He was one of the seven commanders of the seven Ak¶auh∂ƒ∂s of the PµaƒŒava army _(Mahµa., Udyoga.,_ 151). He met his death at the hands of Duryodhana in the Mahµabhµarata War _(Mahµa., ›alya.,_ 12). 

The King of Kµa‹∂ was also a great hero and a Mahµarath∂. His name cannot be clearly made out. In the _Udyoga-Parva_ , Chapter 171, his names are given as Senµavindu and Krodhahantµa. But in the Karƒa-Parva, Chapter 6, where his death is related, his name has been mentioned as Abhibhµu. Purujit and Kuntibhoja were both brothers of Kunt∂, and maternal uncles of Yudhi¶¢hira and his brothers. 

Both of them met their end at the hands of Droƒµacµarya in the Mahµabhµarata War _(Mahµa., Karƒa.,_ 6.22,28). 

›aibya was the father-in-law of the righteous King Yudhi¶¢hira. His daughter Devikµa was given in marriage to Yudhi¶¢hira _(Ådi-Parva,_ 95). He was not only a hero and a powerful fighter, but was also a great man of character. That is why he has been called 'the best of men'. 

Yudhµamanyu and Uttamaujµa were two brothers, who were princes of the Pµa¤cµala territory _(Mahµa., Droƒa.,_ 130). In the formation of the battle array they were posted to guard the wheels of Arjunaís chariot _(Mahµa., Bh∂¶ma.,_ 15-19). They were both great fighters and heroes possessed of immense strength, therefore the two attributes ëmightyí and ëvaliantí have been added to their names. They both met their death at the hands of A‹vatthµamµa while asleep at night _(Mahµa., Sauptika.,_ 8.34,37). 

Abhimanyu was Arjunaís son, born of Subhadrµa, who was ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒaís sister. He was married to Uttarµa, the daughter of Virµa¢a, King of the Matsyas. Abhimanyu received his training in archery from his father, Arjuna, as well as from ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒaís eldest son, Pradyumna, and was a fighter of uncommon merit. On a particular day in the course of the Mahµabhµarata War Droƒµacµarya had adopted the military formation of Cakravyµuha, ëArray of the Wheelí, of such exceptional strength that even the foremost PµaƒŒava warriors like Yudhi¶¢hira, Bh∂ma, Nakula, Sahadeva, Virµa¢a, Drupada and Dhæ¶¢adyumna failed to enter it, being worsted by Jayadratha, who kept the gate. Arjuna was engaged in fighting elsewhere. On that day the young hero Abhimanyu broke into that military formation of the Kaurava army single-handed and gave an exhibition of his exceptional military prowess by killing 

***[Chapter I ] *** 

**39** 

by A‹vatthµamµa at dead of night _(Mahµa., Sauptika_ ., 8). 

innumerable fighters on the side of the enemy. But Droƒa, Kæpµacµarya, Karƒa, A‹vatthµamµa, Bæhadbala and Kætavarmµåó these six Mahµarath∂s of the Kaurava army, in contravention of the rules of war, surrounded the young hero, Abhimanyu, who even in that state despatched, single-handed, many a fighter to his doom. In the end he was struck on the head with a club by Du¨‹µasanaís son, and this caused his death _(Mahµa., Droƒa.,_ 49.). King Par∂k¶it was the son of Abhimanyu. 

A great hero exceptionally well-versed in the scriptures and the science of arms, and capable of commanding ten thousand bowmen battling together is called a Mahµarath∂. ***** Separate and detailed description of the prowess of almost all the fighters whose names have been cited by Duryodhana in these verses can be found in the _Mahµabhµarata, UdyogaParva_ , Chapters 169-172. There also they have been described as either Atirath∂s or Mahµarath∂s. Over and above them, there were other Mahµarath∂s in the PµaƒŒava army, whose names appear in the above chapters of the _Mahµabhµarata_ . The term _Serve_ in verse 6 should be interpreted to include them all. 

The names of the five sons of Draupad∂ were Prativindhya, ›rutasoma, ›rutakarmµa, ›atµan∂ka and ›rutasena, who were descended from the loins of Yudhi¶¢hira, Bh∂masena, Arjuna, Nakula and Sahadeva respectively _(Mahµa., Ådi.,_ 221.80-84). They were killed

**•S◊Ê∑§◊˜** on our side; **ÃÈ** also; **ÁflÁ≥ÊCÔUÊ—** (the) principal (warriors); **ÿ** who (are); **ÃÊŸ˜** them; **ÁŸ’Êœ** know; **Ám¡ÊûÊ◊** O best of Brµahmaƒas, (lit., the twice-born–); **ŸÊÿ∑§Ê—** generals; **◊◊ ‚ÒãÿSÿ** of my army; **‚ÜôÊÊÕ¸◊Ô˜** information; **ÃÊŸ˜** them; **’˝flËÁ◊** I mention (below); **Ã** for you. 

## **O best of Brµahmaƒas, know them also who are the principal warriors on our sideó the generals of my army. For your information, I mention them: (7)** 

The term ëTuí in the text has been used in the sense of ëalsoí. Applying it after ëAsmµakamí (on our side), Duryodhana intends to say that not only in the PµaƒŒava army, but in his own army also, there were many great heroes and fighters. 

The word ëVi‹i¶¢µa¨, has been used to point out those who were supreme in his army in 

point of heroism, steadiness, strength, intellect, courage, prowess, energy and knowledge of arms. The verb ëNibodhaí brings out that there was no paucity of such heroes and fighters of exceptional merit in his own army, and he was citing the names of only a selected few among them for the personal knowledge of the Åcµarya. 

_Now, in two verses, Duryodhana proceeds to mention the names of the principal warriors on his side, and praise them and other heroes of his army._

**÷flÊŸ˜** yourself; **÷Ëc◊—** Bh∂¶ma; **ø** and; **∑§áÊ¸—** Karƒa; **øÔ** and; **∑Î§¬—** Kæpa; **øÔ** and; **‚Á◊ÁÃTÿ—** (ever) victorious in battle; **•EàÕÊ◊Ê** A‹vatthµamµa; **Áfl∑§áÊ¸—** Vikarƒa; **ø** and; **‚ÊÒ◊ŒÁûÊ—** the son of Somadatta, Bhµuri‹ravµa; **ÃÕÊ** so; **∞fl** even; **ø** and. 

**ëëYourself and Bh∂¶ma, Karƒa and Kæpµacµarya, who is ever victorious in battle, and, even so, A‹vatthµamµa, Vikarƒa and Bhµuri‹ravµa (the son of Somadatta)î.  (8)** 

Åcµarya Droƒa was Mahar¶i Bharadvµajaís son. From Mahar¶i Agnivesya and ›r∂ Para‹urµama he obtained knowledge of all forms of arms and missiles with their secrets. A thorough master of Vedas and Vedµa∆gas (sciences helpful in the study of the Vedas)* and a great ascetic, he was an exceptionally bold Atirath∂, possessed of very deep knowledge and experience of archery, and the use of other forms of weapons, and unrivalled skill in warfare. He was fully aware of the use of Brahmµastra, Ågneyµastra and other wonderful weapons of war. Whenever he joined a battle with all his strength, he was unconquerable. He was married to Kæp∂, the daughter of Mahar¶i ›aradvµan, and A‹vatthµamµa was the fruit of this wedlock. He was a friend of King Drupada in his early days. On one occasion when approaching King Drupada he addressed the latter as his dear friend. Drupada, intoxicated with power, rudely hurled the reply to his face, ëëA powerful king like me can never be the friend of a poor beggar like you.íí This rebuke of King Drupada went deep into his heart; thereafter going to Hastinµapura, he began to live under the shelter of his brother-in-law, Kæpµacµarya. There he came in touch with Bh∂¶ma, who appointed him as the teacher of the Kaurava and PµaƒŒava princes. On the conclusion of the training of these princes, Droƒa asked his pupils to pay him the preceptorís fee by 

bringing Drupada before him as a prisoner of war. His favourite pupil Arjuna succeeded in carrying out the behest of his teacher by vanquishing Drupada in battle, and brought him as a prisoner with all the ministers of his Court. Without inflicting any physical injury on him Droƒa released Drupada from his bondage, but deprived him of his territory lying north of the river Bhµag∂rath∂, which he appropriated for himself. In the Mahµabhµarata War, Droƒa carried on a fearful fight as the Commander-in-chief of the Kaurava forces for five days; but in the end hearing a false report about the death of his son he renounced his arms, and sitting in the posture of Yoga entered into deep meditation on God. When his life-force left the mortal frame, his bright soul illumined the entire region of the sky with a brilliant light. At this stage Dhæ¶¢adyumna struck him with a sharp sword, which separated his head from the trunk. 

In the foregoing verse, beginning it with the word ìYourselfî, Duryodhana first cited the name of Droƒa among the heroes on his side, so that Droƒa might be highly pleased with him and enter the battle-field with all the strength and fervour at his command. Moreover, as his teacher, Droƒa was worthy of the honour by Duryodhana, and it was reasonable from this point of view also to count him first among his warriors. 

Bh∂¶ma was the eldest son of King 

***** They are: 1. Vyåkaraƒa (Grammar), 2. Chandas (Prosody), 3. Jyoti¶a (Astronomy), 4. ›ik¶å (Phonetics), 5. Kalpa (the science of rituals), and 6. Nirukta (Etymology). 

***[Chapter I ] *** 

**41** 

›åntanu. He was born of Bhµag∂rath∂ (the spirit of the river Ga∆gµa). He was an incarnation of the ninth Vasu, a celestial named ëDyuí _(Mahµa., ›ånti.,_ 50,26). His original name was, Devavrata. While in the prime of youth, he took the vow of life-long celibacy and renunciation of all claim to the throne in order to facilitate his fatherís marriage with Satyavat∂, in answer to the demand of Satyavat∂ís fosterfather. Owing to the terrible nature of this vow, he became famous on earth as Bh∂¶ma (the superior most). For the sake of his fatherís happiness, he wholly abandoned without the least hesitation the happiness of possessing a wife and a kingdom, which are objects of great temptation to the generality of men in this world. Extremely delighted at this uncommon sacrifice of his, Bh∂¶maís father, ›µantanu, gave him the boon that even Death would be powerless to kill him without his consent. A life-long celibate, the very embodiment of flaming energy, and a master of the scriptures and of the science of warfare, Bh∂¶ma was a great soul, possessed of infinite knowledge and heroism of the highest order and a strength of resolve unsurpassed in its firmness. All the great virtues like valour, renunciation, endurance, forgiveness, compassion, tranquillity, self-control, truthfulness, non-violence, contentment, equanimity, strength, righteousness, majestic bearing, humility, large-heartedness, popularity, straight-forwardness in speech, courage, continence, indifference to worldly attachments, knowledge, wisdom, devotion to parents, knowledge of the scriptures and devotion to the preceptor, etc., were found fully developed in him. Above all, his life was fully saturated with the spirit of devotion to God. He possessed full knowledge of the divinity of Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa, and was an exclusive lover and faithful devotee of the Lord. In the Mahµabhµarata War, he had no equal in the field of battle. He 

made a promise to Duryodhana that though he would refrain from killing the five PµaƒŒavas, he would in course of the fight slaughter with his own hands ten thousand warriors every day _(Mahµa., Udyoga.,_ 156. 21). Holding the position of the Chief Commander of the Kaurava army, he carried on a fearful fight for ten days. Thereafter, lying on his bed of arrows he enlightened all by delivering to them his inexhaustible store of knowledge, and, when the sun started on its northern course, gave up the body of his own free will. 

Karƒa was a son of Kunt∂, the mother of the PµaƒŒavas, begotten of the Sun-god when Kunt∂ was a young maiden. Putting him into a box, Kunt∂ had thrown him into the river; but fortunately this did not cause his death, and carried by the current of the river the box finally reached Hastinµapura. There picked up by Adhiratha, a Sµuta (charioteer) by caste, the child was taken to his home and was nursed and brought up by Rµadhµa, the wife of Adhiratha, and began to be recognized as their own child. Born with a natural gold armour and earrings, he was given the name of ëVasu¶eƒaí, Karƒa received his training in arms from Droƒµacµarya and Para‹urµama, and became a proficient and experienced master of the scriptures and of the science of arms. He was a match for Arjuna in the knowledge of arms and as a fighter in the field. Duryodhana crowned him as King of the A∆gas (territory corresponding to modern Bhagalpur in Bihar). A bosom friend of Duryodhana, he devoted himself body and soul to the constant thought of Duryodhanaís welfare. His devotion to Duryodhana was so deep and great that even though approached by mother Kunt∂ and Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa Himself, he refused to give up the cause of Duryodhana and join the PµaƒŒavas during the fight at Kuruk¶etra. His charities were incomparable. A regular worshipper of the Sun-god, he used to give away with great 

***[Bhagavad-G∂tµa ] *** 

**42** 

pleasure whatever was asked of him, by whosoever it may be, at the time of his worship. One day, in Arjunaís interest, Indra, the King of celestials, assumed the form of a Brµahmaƒa and begged him to make a gift of the natural armour and ear-rings attached to his body. With great delight, that very moment, Karƒa tore his armour and ear rings from the body and gave them away. In exchange for them, Indra gave him a missile, which was infallible in its effect when hurled against a fighter, and with the help of which Karƒa killed Bh∂masenaís son, Gha¢otkaca, during the War. After Droƒµacµaryaís death, assuming the charge of the Kaurava army for two days as Chief Commander, he met his death in a fight with Arjuna. 

Kæpµacµarya is the son of Mahar¶i ›aradvµan of the family of Sage Gotama. He is an experienced master of the science of archery. He had a sister named Kæp∂. Having been brought up by King ›µantanu out of pity (Kæpµa), he got the name of Kæpa, and his sister got the name of Kæp∂. A proficient scholar of the Vedas, he is a virtuous soul possessed of many good qualities and rectitude of conduct. Before the advent of Droƒµacµarya, he used to impart instruction in the science of archery to the Kaurava, PµaƒŒava and Yµadava warriors. He remained alive even after the death of all the Kauravas, and it was he who imparted the knowledge of arms to King Par∂k¶it. He is a great hero and a great expert in vanquishing an enemy; therefore, the attribute of ëSamiti¤jaya¨í, (ever victorious in battle) has been affixed to his name in the verse. He is 

said to be still alive. 

A‹vatthµamµa is the son of Åcµarya Droƒa. A great expert in military science, and a great fighter and hero, he is counted as a Mahµarath∂. He also received his training in arms under his father, Droƒµacµarya. He too is believed to be alive still. 

Vikarƒa was one of the hundred sons of Dhætarµa¶¢ra. He was an extremely virtuous soul, a great hero and a Mahµarath∂. At the time of her persecution in the Kaurava Court when Draupad∂ asked the assembled people whether she had been actually lost to the PµaƒŒavas as a stake in the gamble, except Vidura there appeared none to open his lips against that persecution. At that critical moment, it was Vikarƒa alone who rose in the open Court and declared in scathing terms, upholding both justice and righteousness, that it was a great injustice not to answer Draupad∂ís question. He also said that in his opinion Draupad∂ had not been won by the Kauravas as a stake in the game _(Mahµabhµarata, SabhµaParva._ 63.18-25). 

Bhµuri‹ravµa, the son of Somadatta, was the grandson of King ›µantanuís elder brother, Bµahl∂ka. He too was a virtuous soul, an expert in the art of warfare, a great hero and a Mahµarath∂. He performed many sacrifices giving away huge riches as sacrificial fees. He met his death at the hands of Sµatyaki in the Mahµabhµarata War. 

The two indeclinables ëTathµaí and ëEvaí used at the end of the verse, show that equally with Kæpµacµarya, A‹vatthµamµa, Vikarƒa and Bhµuri‹ravµa too were ëever victorious in battleí. 

**•ãÿ ø ’„Ufl— ≥ÊÍ⁄UÊ ◊ŒÕ¸ àÿQ§¡ËÁflÃÊ—– ŸÊŸÊ≥ÊÔSòÊ¬˝„U⁄UáÊÊ— ‚fl¸ ÿÈhÁfl≥ÊÊ⁄UŒÊ—H 9H** 

**•ãÿ** other; **ø** and; **’„Ufl—** many; **≥ÊÍ⁄UÊ—** heroes; **◊ŒÕ¸** for me; **àÿQ§¡ËÁflÃÊ—** who have staked 

***[Chapter I ] *** 

**43** 

> (their) lives; **ŸÊŸÊ≥ÊSòÊ¬˝„U⁄UáÊÊ—** equipped with various weapons and missiles; **‚fl¸** all; **ÿÈhÁfl≥ÊÊ⁄UŒÊ—** skilled in warfare. 

**And there are many other heroes, all skilled in warfare, equipped with various weapons and missiles, who have staked their lives for me.                   (9)** 

The names of ›alya, Bµahl∂ka, Bhagadatta, Kætavarmå, Jayadratha and other Mahµarath∂s do not appear in any verse going before; therefore pointing to them all in a general way in the present verse, Duryodhana intends to show that the names of heroes taken by him did not exhaust the list of the heroes on his side, but that besides them there were many other fighters who stood on his side, well- 

equipped with arms like swords, clubs, etc., which are wielded by the hand, and missiles like arrows, iron clubs, spears etc., who were Mahµarath∂s and great experts in the art of warfare, and were ready to sacrifice their lives for him. He wanted to assure the Åcµarya that all these warriors would fight for the victory of his side to the best of their ability till their very last breath. 

_After praising the prowess of the great warriors on his side Duryodhana now proceeds to compare the two armies, and declares his own army as more powerful than and superior to that of the PµaƒŒavas._ 

## **•¬ÿÊ¸#¢ ÃŒS◊Ê∑¢§ ’∂¢U ÷Ëc◊ÊÁ÷⁄UÁˇÊÃ◊Ô˜– ¬ÿÊ¸#¢ ÁàflŒ◊Ã·Ê¢ ’∂¢U ÷Ë◊ÊÁ÷⁄UÁˇÊÃ◊˜ÔH 10H** 

**•¬ÿÊ¸#◊˜Ô** (is) unconquerable; **ÃÃ˜Ô** this; **•S◊Ê∑§◊˜Ô** of ours; **’∂U◊˜Ô** army; **÷Ëc◊ÊÁ÷⁄UÁˇÊÃ◊˜Ô** fully protected by Bh∂¶ma; **¬ÿÊ¸#◊˜Ô** (is) easy to conquer; **ÃÈ** while; **ßŒ◊˜Ô** that; **∞Ã·Ê◊˜Ô** of theirs; **’∂U◊˜Ô** army; **÷Ë◊ÊÁ÷⁄UÁˇÊÃ◊˜Ô** guarded in every way by Bh∂ma. 

**This army of ours, fully protected by Bh∂¶ma, is unconquerable; while that army of theirs, guarded in every way by Bh∂ma, is easy to conquer. (10)** 

By declaring his army as protected by Bh∂¶ma and unconquerable, Duryodhana proves the superiority of his forces. The point he made was that the army collected by him was full of many renowned Generals or Mahµarath∂s, and it was under the protection of Bh∂¶ma, the greatest living hero and fighter of the world, who had frustrated even the great Para‹urµama in battle. From the numerical point of view as well, his army comprised four Ak¶auhiƒ∂s more than the PµaƒŒava army. It was not possible, therefore, for anybody to vanquish such an army, which possessed more power than what was required for the purpose of vanquishing the PµaƒŒavas. In the _Mahµabhµarata, Udyogaparva_ 

Chapter 55, Duryodhana similarly gives a description of his army to Dhætarµa¶¢ra, where also he mentions almost all those Mahµarath∂s by name, and declaring that the great Bh∂¶ma was its protector shows its invincibility. There he clearly says, 'O King, I find the majority of fighters in the ranks of the enemy without any material qualities, and the majority of fighters in our ranks as possessed of remarkable qualities and of great promise'. ***** 

Therefore, in his opinion there was no cause to fear defeat in the War. Similarly, in the _Bh∂¶ma-Parva_ , where Duryodhana, again, gives a description of his army to Droƒµacµarya, he merely repeats the above verse of the G∂tµa _(Bh∂¶ma-Parva_ , 51.6). In 

*** ªÈáÊ„UËŸ¢ ¬⁄U·Ê¢ ø ’„ÈU ¬≥ÿÊÁ◊ ÷Ê⁄UÃ– ªÈáÊÊŒÿ¢ ’„ÈUªÈáÊ◊Êà◊Ÿp Áfl≥ÊÊê¬ÃH** 

***[Bhagavad-G∂tµa ] *** 

**44** 

the verse which precedes it, he goes so far as to say, ëëAll of you Mahµarath∂s possess the power to kill the PµaƒŒavas with their entire army single-handed; it should cause no wonder, then, if you kill them with your combined efforts.í ***** 

From all these facts it is clear that the term ëAparyµaptaí in the above verse is used by Duryodhana to bring out the strength and greatness of his army. Wherever these verses have been uttered, as in the places referred to above, their intention is to give encouragement to the warriors on his side. The propriety of this interpretation is proved by the context in which the verse is uttered by Duryodhana in every case. 

By declaring the PµaƒŒava army as 

guarded by Bh∂ma and ëParyµaptaí (easy to conquer), Duryodhana attempted to prove it to be a weaker and vulnerable army. The point he sought to make clear was that whereas Bh∂¶ma was the protector of the Kaurava army, there stood on the other side Bh∂ma, who though possessed of a strong body, could not stand comparison with Bh∂¶ma as a fighter. So far as mastery of the art of warfare, knowledge of arms and scriptures, and power of intellect were concerned, Bh∂¶ma was quite incomparable; whereas Bh∂ma was, if anything, an indifferent archer, and a man of dull intellect. Therefore, according to Duryodhana, the PµaƒŒava army was ëParyµaptaíólimited in power, and easy to be conquered by the Kauravas. 

_Thus declaring his army, protected by Bh∂¶ma, as unconquerable, Duryodhana now proceeds to exhort all his Generals, including Droƒµacµarya, to guard Bh∂¶ma from every direction._ 

## **•ÿŸ·È ø ‚fl¸·È ÿÕÊ÷Êª◊flÁSÕÃÊ—– ÷Ëc◊◊flÊÁ÷⁄UˇÊãÃÈ ÷flãÃ— ‚fl¸ ∞fl Á„UH 11H** 

**•ÿŸ·È** fronts; **ø** and; **‚fl¸·È** on all; **ÿÕÊ÷Êª◊˜Ô** in (your) respective positions; **•flÁSÕÃÊ—Ô** stationed; **÷Ëc◊◊˜Ô** Bh∂¶ma; **∞fl** indeed; **•Á÷⁄UˇÊãÃÈÔ** protect on all sides; **÷flãÃ—Ô** you; **‚fl¸Ô** all; **∞flÔ** in particular; **Á„UÔ** positively. 

**Therefore, stationed in your respective positions on all fronts, do you all guard Bh∂¶ma in particular on all sides.                                          (11)** 

The great warrior Bh∂¶ma possessed the power to protect himself: Duryodhana too knew this fact full well. But Bh∂¶ma had made an announcement that Drupadaís son ›ikhaƒŒ∂ had taken birth as a female child and subsequently became a male through sex-transformation; but because he was born as a female, Bh∂¶ma still regarded him as a female, and would not face him in battle according to the canons of chivalry followed by a K¶atriya fighter. That is why on a previous occasion also while reviewing the 

army, Duryodhana had cautioned Du¨‹µasana and all other warriors by explaining this danger in detail _(Mahµa., Bh∂¶ma_ 15.14-20). Similarly, on the present occasion impelled by that lurking fear in his mind, Duryodhana exhorts all the principal generals in his army to maintain, with their utmost strength, their respective positions in the battle-array, taking exceptional care and precaution that ›ikhaƒŒ∂ might not get a loophole to come in front of Bh∂¶ma in the course of the battle. They should be alert to drive away ›ikhaƒŒ∂ by 

*** ∞∑Ò§∑§≥Ê— ‚◊ÕÊ¸ Á„U ÿÍÿ¢ ‚fl¸ ◊„UÊ⁄UÕÊ—– ¬Êá«ÈU¬ÈòÊÊŸ˜Ô ⁄UáÊ „UãÃÈ¢ ‚‚ÒãÿÊŸ˜Ô Á∑§◊È ‚¢„UÃÊ—H** 

***[Chapter I ] *** 

**45** 

force of arms, the moment he made any such attempt. If Bh∂¶ma could be saved from ›ikhaƒŒ∂, there was nothing else for the 

Kauravas to fear; for it was quite easy for Bh∂¶ma to vanquish single-handed all the Mahµarath∂s in the PµaƒŒava army. 

_After the above account of how Duryodhana praised the principal warriors of his army, and more particularly his Chief Commander Bh∂¶ma, Sa¤jaya now proceeds to describe the subsequent events on the field of battle:_ 

**ÃSÿ ‚TŸÿã„U·Z ∑È§L§flÎh— Á¬ÃÊ◊„U—– Á‚¢„UŸÊŒ¢ ÁflŸlÊìÊÒ— ≥ÊÔW¢ Œä◊ÊÒ ¬˝ÃÊ¬flÊŸ˜H 12H ÃSÿÔ** to him (Duryodhana); **‚TŸÿŸ˜Ô** bringing; **„U·¸◊˜Ô** joy; **∑È§L§flÎh—** the grand old man of the Kaurava race; **Á¬ÃÊ◊„U—Ô** (their) grand-uncle (Bh∂¶ma); **Á‚¢„UŸÊŒ◊˜Ô** like a lion; **ÁflŸlÔ** roaring; **©UìÊÒ—Ô** terribly; **≥ÊW◊˜Ô** (his) conch; **Œä◊ÊÒ** blew; **¬˝ÃÊ¬flÊŸ˜Ô** glorious. 

**The grand old man of the Kaurava race, their glorious grand-patriarch, Bh∂¶ma, cheering up Duryodhana, roared terribly like a lion and blew his conch.    (12)** 

Next to Bµahl∂ka, Bh∂¶ma was the oldest member of the Kaurava race. He had the same degree of relationship with the Kauravas and the PµaƒŒavas, and being the grand-uncle of both the branches, he was an object of reverence to both; that is why Sa¤jaya called him ëthe grand old maní of the Kauravas, and grand-uncle. Though much advanced in age, he excelled even the foremost younger heroes in energy, strength, fitness and heroism; therefore, he has been described as ëgloriousí. The grand old warrior noticed Duryodhana standing near Droƒµacµarya, 

somewhat startled and anxious at the sight of the PµaƒŒava array. He further noticed that suppressing his anxiety, Duryodhana had been praising the Kaurava army in order to encourage the fighters and had been exhorting Droƒa and the other Mahµarath∂s to protect Bh∂¶ma. Intending, therefore, to assure Duryodhana of his own great prowess, and delight Duryodhanaís heart, and to proclaim, as Chief Commander, the commencement of the fight, Bh∂¶ma gave a loud roar like a lion, and blew his conch with great force. 

**ÃÃ— ≥ÊÔWÊp ÷ÿ¸p ¬áÊflÊŸ∑§ªÊ◊ÈπÊ—– ‚„U‚ÒflÊèÿ„UãÿãÃ ‚ ≥ÊéŒSÃÈ◊È∂UÊ˘÷flÃ˜H 13H ÃÃ—** then; **≥ÊÔWÊ—Ô** conches; **ø** and; **÷ÿ¸—** kettledrums; **øÔ** and; **¬áÊflÊŸ∑§ªÊ◊ÈπÊ—Ô** tabors, drums and trumpets; **‚„U‚Ê ∞fl** all at once; **•èÿ„UãÿãÃÔ** blared forth; **‚ ≥ÊÔéŒ—** (and) the noise; **ÃÈ◊È∂U—** tumultuous; **•÷flÃ˜** was. 

## **Then conches, kettledrums, tabors, drums and trumpets all at once blared forth and the noise was tumultuous.                                           (13)** 

When roaring like a lion and blowing his conch, Bh∂¶ma announced the commencement of the battle, all the regions were flooded with hope and encouragement, and suddenly in all sections of the army conches, trumpets and other instruments of 

martial music, belonging to the different commanders, were sounded. The sounding of these instruments at one and the same time produced terrible noise that echoed and re-echoed through the entire region of the sky. 

***[Bhagavad-G∂tµa ] *** 

**46** 

_Dhætarµa¶¢raís query was: 'After assembling for the fight, what did his own sons and the sons of PµaƒŒu do?' In answer to this, Sa¤jaya described up to this point the doings of the fighters on Dhætarµa¶¢raís side. And now, in five verses, he proceeds to describe the doings of the PµaƒŒavas:_ 

## **ÃÃ— EÃÒ„¸UÿÒÿ¸ÈQ ◊„UÁÃ SÿãŒŸ ÁSÕÃÊÒ– ◊Êœfl— ¬Êá«UflpÒfl ÁŒ√ÿÊÒ ≥ÊÔWÊÒ ¬˝Œä◊ÃÈ—H 14H** 

**ÃÃ—** then; **EÃÒ— „UÿÒ— ÿÈQ  ◊„UÁÃ SÿãŒŸÔ** in a glorious chariot drawn by white horses; **ÁSÕÃÊÒÔ** seated; **◊Êœfl—** ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa (lit., Lord of Lak¶m∂, the Goddess of Prosperity); **¬Êá«Ufl—Ô** the (celebrated) son of PµaƒŒu, Arjuna; **ø** and; **∞fl** also; **ÁŒ√ÿÊÒ ≥ÊÔWÊÒÔ** (their) celestial conches; **¬˝Œä◊ÃÈ—** blew. 

**Then, seated in a glorious chariot drawn by white horses, ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa as well as Arjuna blew their celestial conches.                                    (14)** 

Arjunaís chariot was very large and excellent in every way. Covered all over with a plate of gold, it looked exceptionally bright and beautiful, and was very strong in build. Flags decorated it on all sides with small tinkling bells attached to them. The wheels attached to it were large and strong. A high flag containing the emblems of the moon and stars glittered like lightning and ›r∂ Hanumµan was posted on it. With regard to this flag Sa¤jayaís report to Duryodhana was that in height as well as in length it covered the distance of a Yojana (8 miles). Its colour was as variegated as the rainbow in a cloud. Though so large and widely extended, it was very light, and could not be held up by any obstruction. It passed easily through clusters of trees without ever being touched by them. 

There were four celestial horses attached to this chariot, all white, very beautiful, welldecorated, well-trained, strong and nimble. These were taken from the hundred celestial horses received as a gift from the Gandharva 

king, Citraratha. The peculiarity about these horses was that any number of them might be killed in action, and yet their total number would ever remain hundred and could not be reduced. Moreover, they could go anywhere on this earth and in heaven. This was also true of the chariot _(Mahµa., Udyoga.,_ 56), which was a gift made to Arjuna by the Firegod as a mark of pleasure after the burning of the KhµaƒŒava forest _(Mahµa., Ådi.,_ 225). Seated on this glorious chariot, when Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa and the great warrior Arjuna heard the tumultuous noise produced by the blowing of conches and sounding of drums and other instruments of martial music by the fighters of the Kaurava army including the great Bh∂¶ma, they too, in order to announce the commencement of the fight, blew their respective conches. The conches belonging to Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa and Arjuna were no ordinary conches; they were of an extraordinary type, brilliant to look at and most uncommon in character. That is why they have been called ëcelestial conchesí.

***[Chapter I ] *** 

**47** 

> Devadatta; **œŸTÿ—** Arjuna; **¬ÊÒá«˛U◊˜** known as PauƒŒra; **Œä◊ÊÒ** blew; **◊„UÊ≥ÊÔW◊˜** (his) mighty conch; **÷Ë◊∑§◊Ê¸** of valiant deeds; **flÎ∑§ÊŒ⁄U—** Bh∂ma (the second son of PµaƒŒu). 

## **›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa blew His conch named Pµa¤cajanya; Arjuna, his own called Devadatta; while Bh∂ma of terrific valiant deeds blew his mighty conch, PauƒŒra. (15)** 

ëHæ¶∂kaí, means a sense, and the Lord (Controller or Propeller) of the senses is called ëHæ¶∂ke‹aí*. The term also denotes a repository of joy, happiness and power accompanied with amenities of life.– The Lord is not only the controller of the senses, but a repository of joy, happiness and power accompanied with amenities of life. Therefore one of the names of Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa is ëHæ¶∂ke‹a.í Having killed a demon, Pa¤cajana by name, who possessed the form of a conch, Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa took him for His conch. That is why His Conch got the name of ëPµa¤cajanyaí (Hariva≈‹a II. xxxiii.17). 

x x x 

At the time of the Rµajasµuya sacrifice, Arjuna conquered a large number of kingdoms and brought untold riches to the PµaƒŒava capital; that is how he got the epithet of ëDhana¤jayaí. And he obtained the conch 

named ëDevadattaí from Indra, the King of Heaven, when Arjuna went there to fight the Nivµatakavacas and other demons _(Mahµa., Vana.,_ 174,5). The sound of this conch was so loud and terrible that it used to terrify the soldiers in the enemyís ranks. 

x x x 

The second PµaƒŒava, Bh∂ma, was a man possessing exceptional physical strength. His deeds used to be so terrible that they caused terror in the heart of those who either saw or heard of them. That is how he came to be known as ëBh∂ma, of terrific valiant deedsí. He was a great eater and possessed extraordinary power to digest a huge quantity of food; that is how he got the name of ëVækodaraí. He possessed a conch of very large size, whose sound reverberated to a long distance; hence it has been called a ëmighty conch.í 

## **•ŸãÃÁfl¡ÿ¢ ⁄UÊ¡Ê ∑È§ãÃË¬ÈòÊÊ ÿÈÁœÁDÔU⁄U—– Ÿ∑È§‹— ‚„UŒflp ‚ÈÉÊÊ·◊ÁáÊ¬Èc¬∑§ÊÒH 16H** 

**•ŸãÃÁfl¡ÿ◊˜** conch known by the name of Anantavijaya; **⁄UÊ¡Ê** king; **∑È§ãÃË¬ÈòÊ—** (the eldest) son of Kunt∂ (aunt of ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa); **ÿÈÁœÁDÔU⁄U—** Yudhi¶¢hira (lit., steadfast in battle); **Ÿ∑È§‹—** Nakul (the fourth son of PµaƒŒu); **‚„UŒfl—** Sahadeva (the fifth son of PµaƒŒu); **ø** and; **‚ÈÉÊÊ·◊ÁáÊ¬Èc¬∑§ÊÒ** (their) conches Sugho¶a and Maƒipu¶paka (respectively). 

## **King Yudhi¶¢hira, son of Kunt∂, blew his conch, Anantavijaya; while Nakula and Sahadeva blew theirs, known as Sugho¶a and Maƒipu¶paka, respectively. (16)** 

Of the five sons of PaƒŒu, Yudhi¶¢hira, Bh∂ma and Arjuna were born of his first wife Kunt∂, and Nakula and Sahadeva, of Mµadr∂, the second wife. In the present verse, the names of Nakula and Sahadeva also appear; and in order to show that Yudhi¶¢hira, 

Nakula and Sahadeva were not sons of the same mother, Yudhi¶¢hira has been referred to in the verse as the ëson of Kunt∂.í Though at the time of the War Yudhi¶¢hira possessed no kingdom, he had conquered all the kings at the time of the Rµajasµuya sacrifice, and

**∑§Ê≥ÿp ¬⁄U◊cflÊ‚— Á≥ÊÔπá«UË ø ◊„UÊ⁄UÕ—– œÎCÔUlÈ◊AÊ Áfl⁄UÊ≈Up ‚ÊàÿÁ∑§pÊ¬⁄UÊÁ¡Ã—H 17H º˝È¬ŒÊ º˝ÊÒ¬ŒÿÊp ‚fl¸≥ÊÔ— ¬ÎÁÕflË¬Ã– ‚ÊÒ÷º˝p ◊„UÊ’Ê„ÈU— ≥ÊÔWÊãŒä◊È— ¬ÎÕÄ¬ÎÕ∑˜§ÔH 18H ∑§Ê≥ÿ—** (the) King of Kµa‹∂ (the modern Vµarµaƒas∂); **ø** and; **¬⁄U◊cflÊ‚—** (the) excellent archer; **Á≥ÊÔπá«UË** ›ikhaƒŒ∂; **ø** and; **◊„UÊ⁄UÕ—** (the) great car-warrior; **œÎCÔUlÈ◊A—** Dhæ¶¢adyumna; **Áfl⁄UÊ≈U—** King Virµa¢a; **ø** and; **‚ÊàÿÁ∑§—** Sµatyaki; **ø** and; **•¬⁄UÊÁ¡Ã—** invincible; **º˝È¬Œ—** King Drupada; **º˝ÊÒ¬ŒÿÊ—** the five sons of Draupad∂; **ø** as well as; **‚fl¸≥ÊÔ—** all of these and from all sides; **¬ÎÁÕflË¬Ã** O lord of the earth; **‚ÊÒ÷º˝—** (the) son of Subhadrµa, Abhimanyu; **ø** and; **◊„UÊ’Ê„ÈU—** mightyarmed; **≥ÊÔWÊŸ˜** (their respective) conches; **Œä◊È—** blew; **¬ÎâÊ∑˜ ¬ÎâÊ∑˜** severally. **And the excellent archer, the King of Kµa‹∂, and ›ikhaƒŒ∂, the Mahµarath∂ (great chariot-warrior), Dhæ¶¢adyumna and Virµa¢a; and invincible Sµatyaki, Drupada as well as the five sons of Draupad∂, and the mighty-armed Abhimanyu, son of Subhadrµa, all of them, O Lord of the earth, severally blew their respective conches from all sides.  (17-18)** 

›ikhaƒŒ∂ and Dhæ¶¢adyumna both were sons of King Drupada. ›ikhaƒŒ∂ was the elder, and Dhæ¶¢adyumna the younger brother. In the beginning, King Drupada had no issue; he, therefore, resorted to the worship of Bhagavµan ›iva with the motive of obtaining a successor. When Lord ›iva pleased with his worship asked Drupada to take a boon, King Drupada submitted his prayer for a child. Bhagavµan ›iva said he would get a daughter; but Drupada replied that he sought a son, and not a daughter. Thereupon Bhagavµan ›iva said that the girl would subsequently be transformed into a son. As the result of this boon, a daughter was born to King Drupada in course of time; but possessed as he was of full faith in the words of Bhagavµan ›iva, he announced it as the birth of a son. The queen also took precaution to suppress the truth about the childís sex. The daughter was given a boyís name **ë** ›ikhaƒŒ∂, and, dressed as a boy, was given all the requisite education and 

training of a Prince. In due time, that so-called ëPrinceí was married to the daughter of Hiraƒyavarmµa, king of the Da‹µarƒas. Coming to live with her husband the daughter of Hiraƒyavarmµa discovered that ›ikhaƒŒ∂ was a woman, and sad at heart sent word to her father to that effect. King Hiraƒyavarmµa got so enraged at the news that he immediately declared war on King Drupada, and made a resolve to avenge the wrong by taking Drupadaís life. Desiring to avoid the fight, King Drupada took recourse to the worship of the Deity. And ›ikhaƒŒ∂, for his part, terribly cast down at the unfortunate turn of events, quietly left the palace determined to put an end to his life in the forest. There he happened to meet a Yak¶a, Sthµuƒµakarƒa by name, possessed of supernatural power, who out of pity gave his manhood to ›ikhaƒŒ∂ for a stipulated period, accepting for himself ›ikhaƒŒ∂ís femininity in exchange. This is the story of how ›ikhaƒŒ∂ turned a man from a 

***[Chapter I ] *** 

**49** 

woman. Returning thereafter to his kingdom, ›ikhaƒŒ∂ gave assurance to his parents that he was no longer a woman, and pacified his father-in-law, Hiraƒyavarmµa, by furnishing proof of his manhood. By a curse of Kubera, Sthµuƒµakarƒa remained a woman all his life; therefore, ›ikhaƒŒ∂ had not to return his manhood, and remained a man for the rest of his life, Bh∂¶ma was aware of this history of ›ikhaƒŒ∂, and, therefore, always refused to strike him as an adversary. ›ikhaƒŒ∂ was a great hero and fighter, and a Mahµarath∂ among warriors. Placing him in front as a 

screen, Arjuna struck at Bh∂¶ma on the last day of Bh∂¶maís fight, and put an end to the career of that old warrior. The other heroes mentioned in these two verses have already been introduced to the reader. 

x x x 

By the use of the word ëSarvasa¨í in this verse, Sa¤jaya intends to convey that besides ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa the five PµaƒŒavas, the King of Kµa‹∂ and other heroes whose names are mentioned in the above verses, the other Rath∂s, Mahµarath∂s and Atirath∂s in the PµaƒŒava army also blew their respective conches. 

_Having told how following the lead of Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa and Arjuna, all heroes and warriors of the PµaƒŒava army blew their conches from their respective positions, Sa¤jaya now proceeds to show the effect produced by that sound:_ **‚ ÉÊÊ·Ê œÊÃ¸⁄UÊc≈˛UÊáÊÊ¢ NŒÿÊÁŸ √ÿŒÊ⁄UÿÃ˜– Ÿ÷p ¬ÎÁÕflË¥ øÒfl ÃÈ◊È∂UÊ √ÿŸÈŸÊŒÿŸ˜H 19H ‚—** that; **ÉÊÊ·—** sound; **œÊÃ¸⁄UÊc≈˛UÊáÊÊ◊˜** of the sons of Dhætarµa¶¢ra and those who sided with them; **NUŒÿÊÁŸ** (the) hearts; **√ÿŒÊ⁄UÿÃ˜** rent; **Ÿ÷—** heaven; **ø** and; **¬ÎÁâÊflË◊˜** earth; **ø** and; **∞fl** as well; **ÃÈ◊È∂—** terrible; **√ÿŸÈŸÊŒÿŸ˜** echoing through. 

**And the terrible sound, echoing through heaven and earth, rent the hearts of Dhætarµa¶¢raís sons.                                                     (19)** 

When the conches of all the heroes in the PµaƒŒava army were sounded all at a time, the sound produced by them was so great, loud, deep and dreadful that it filled all the regions of heavan and earth. Widely spreading over those regions, it produced echoes, 

which resounded throughout the earth and sky and caused such a terror and fright in the sons of Dhætarµa¶¢ra and the other fighters on their side that they felt a sudden oppression in their hearts as if they had been rent asunder. 

_After describing how the sound of conches blown by the PµaƒŒavas oppressed the hearts of the Kaurava heores, Sa¤jaya proceeds in the next four verses to quote the words which Arjuna, full of enthusiasm, addressed to Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa:ó_ **•Õ √ÿflÁSÕÃÊãŒÎ¶ÔUÊ œÊÃ¸⁄UÊc≈˛UÊŸ˜Ô ∑§Á¬äfl¡—–**

**50** 

**•Õ** now; **√ÿflÁSÕÃÊŸ˜Ô** arrayed (against him); **ŒÎ¶ÔUÊ** seeing; **œÊÃ¸⁄UÊc≈˛ÊŸ˜Ô** the sons of Dhætarµa¶¢ra; **∑§Á¬äfl¡—** an epithet of Arjuna (lit., one who has a monkey seated on his banner); **¬˝flÎûÊ ≥ÊSòÊ‚ê¬ÊÃ** when missiles were ready to be hurled; **œŸÈ—** (his) bow; **©Ulêÿ** taking up; **¬Êá«Ufl—** son of PµaƒŒu, Arjuna; **N·Ë∑§≥Ê◊˜Ô** to ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa; **ÃŒÊ** then; **flÊÄÿ◊˜Ô** words; **ßŒ◊˜Ô** (the) following; **•Ê„U** addressed; **◊„UË¬Ã** O lord of the earth; **‚ŸÿÊ— ©÷ÿÊ—** of the two armies; **◊äÿ** in the middle; **⁄UÕ◊˜Ô** chariot; **SÕÊ¬ÿ** place; **◊** my; **•ëÿÈÃ** O Kæ¶ƒa. 

**Now, O lord of the earth, seeing your sons arrayed against him, and when missiles were ready to be hurled, Arjuna, who had the figure of Hanum** µ **an on the flag of his chariot, took up his bow and then addressed the following words to ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa: ëëKæ¶ƒa, place my chariot between the two armies. (20-21)** 

In accordance with his promise to Bh∂masena _(Mahµa., Vana.,_ 151.17-18) the great hero Hanumµan always occupied the huge flag of Arjuna, and from time to time, during the war, used to give loud and dreadful roars _(Mahµa., Bh∂¶ma.,_ 52.18). Sa¤jaya employs the attribute ëKapidhvajaí for Arjuna, in this verse, to remind Dhætarµa¶¢ra of this fact. 

Observing that Duryodhana and his brothers, and all other Kaurava warriors, in their full battle-uniform, were completely ready with their weapons to start the battle, the heroic sentiment was awakened in Arjunaís mind as well, and he immediately took up the GµaƒŒ∂va bow in his hand. This is what Sa¤jaya tries to convey by verse 20. Referring, again, to Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa 

as ëHæ¶∂ke‹aí in verse 21 above, Sa¤jaya is pointing out to King Dhætarµa¶¢ra that God Himself, the Knower of all hearts, was acting as Arjunaís charioteer. Was it, therefore, not the height of ignorance and folly to expect victory in that fight in which Lord Himself was helping the other side ? 

ëAcyutaí means one who is never vanquished, or who never suffers a fall. The word also means he who ever remains established in his self, and is never dissociated from his power and glory. Addressing ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa by this name, Arjuna reveals his knowledge about the glory and reality of ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa. In other words, Arjuna means to say that regardless of the servile role of driving his chariot, He is nevertheless, and for ever, God Himself! 

**ÿÊflŒÃÊÁÛÊ⁄UËˇÊ˘„¢U ÿÊhÈ∑§Ê◊ÊŸflÁSÕÃÊŸ˜– ∑Ò§◊¸ÿÊ ‚„U ÿÊh√ÿ◊ÁS◊ã⁄UáÊ‚◊Èl◊H 22H ÿÊflÃ˜** till; **∞ÃÊŸ˜** these (adversaries); **ÁŸ⁄UËˇÊ** have carefully observed; **•„U◊˜** I; **ÿÊhÈ∑§Ê◊ÊŸ˜ •flÁSÕÃÊŸ˜** drawn up for battle; **∑Ò§—** whom; **◊ÿÊ** I have to; **‚„U** with; **ÿÊh√ÿ◊˜** engage; **•ÁS◊Ÿ˜ ⁄UáÊ‚◊Èl◊** in this fight. 

**ëëAnd keep it there till I have carefully observed these warriors drawn up for battle, and have seen with whom I have to engage in this fight.            (22)** 

Arjuna says to Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa that taking the chariot between the two armies it should be placed at such a suitable point, and for such length of time, that he could see and closely examine all the warriors arrayed for 

battle in their battle-dress. The object of his making this request was to know definitely who were the heroes on the other side with whom he would be required personally to come to grips in that dreadful business of war. 

***[Chapter I ] *** 

**51** 

## **ÿÊàSÿ◊ÊŸÊŸflˇÊ˘„¢U ÿ ∞Ã˘òÊ ‚◊ÊªÃÊ—– œÊÃ¸⁄UÊc≈˛USÿ ŒÈ’¸Èhÿ¸Èh Á¬˝ÿÁø∑§Ë·¸fl—H 23H** 

**ÿÊàSÿ◊ÊŸÊŸ˜** ready to fight; **•flˇÊ** shall scan (them) **; •„U◊˜** I; **ÿ ∞Ã** whosoever; **•òÊ** here, on this side; **‚◊ÊªÃÊ—** have assembled; **œÊÃ¸⁄UÊc≈˛USÿ** Duryodhana (son of Dhætarµa¶¢ra); **ŒÈ’È¸h—** evil-minded; **ÿÈh** In (this) war; **Á¬˝ÿÁø∑§Ë·¸fl—** well-wishers. 

**ëëI shall have a look at the well-wishers of evil-minded Duryodhana in this war, whoever have assembled on his side and are ready for the fight.íí                  (23)** 

It had been definitely stipulated that on the completion of thirteen yearsí exile, the kingdom of the PµaƒŒavas would be returned to them. These thirteen years the kingdom had remained under the control of the Kauravas only as a trust; but Duryodhana, with the evil intent of wrongfully appropriating the kingdom, backed out of the mutually agreed stipulation altogether. From the beginning of his career up till then Duryodhana had practised many forms of persecution against the PµaƒŒavas, but this last wrong action on his part became wholly intolerable. Remembering this evil intent of Duryodhana, Arjuna speaks of him in the verse as ëevil-mindedí. 

Arjunaís desire to scan the well-wishers of Duryodhana seems to indicate the thought that even though the wrongs and cruelties perpetrated by evilminded Duryodhana were 

well-known throughout the world, those kings had assembled to uphold his cause and help him in the fight. It proved that their mind and intellect had become as vitiated as that of Duryodhana. That is why they had gathered together to lend their support openly to his wrongs, and give him encouragement by the show of their pomp and power. Thus trying to advance Duryodhanaís interest they were, in reality, doing him harm. Arjuna, therefore, wanted to see with his own eyes who those fighters were, who regarding themselves to be great heroes and eager to participate in the war had taken their stand on the battle-field. There was the spirit of challenge in Arjunaís mind when he expressed this desire, and he wanted to teach them a lesson that it did not pay to take side with wrong and unrighteousness. 

_Sa¤jaya now proceeds to describe in two verses what Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa did on hearing the above request of Arjuna._ 

_‚Tÿ ©UflÊø_ **∞fl◊ÈQÔ§Ê N·Ë∑§≥ÊÔÊ ªÈ«UÊ∑§≥ÊÔŸ ÷Ê⁄UÃ– ‚ŸÿÊL§÷ÿÊ◊¸äÿ SÕÊ¬ÁÿàflÊ ⁄UÕÊûÊ◊◊˜H 24H ÷Ëc◊º˝ÊáÊ¬˝◊ÈπÃ— ‚fl¸·Ê¢ ø ◊„UËÁˇÊÃÊ◊˜– ©UflÊø ¬ÊÕ¸ ¬≥ÿÒÃÊã‚◊flÃÊã∑È§M§ÁŸÁÃH 25H** 

**∞fl◊˜** thus; **©UQÔ§—** addressed; **N·Ë∑§≥Ê—Ô** ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa; **ªÈ«UÊ∑§≥ÊÔŸ** by Arjuna; **÷Ê⁄UÃ** O Dhætarµa¶¢ra (lit., a descendant of Bharata); **‚ŸÿÊ— ©U÷ÿÊ—Ô** of the two armies; **◊äÿ** in the middle; **SÕÊ¬ÁÿàflÊÔ** having placed; **⁄UÕÊûÊ◊◊˜** the magnificent chariot; **÷Ëc◊º˝ÊáÊ¬˝◊ÈπÃ—** in front of Bhi¶ma and Droƒa; **‚fl¸·Ê◊˜** all; **ø** and; **◊„UËÁˇÊÃÊ◊˜** (in front) of the kings; **©UflÊø** said; **¬ÊÕ¸** O (celebrated) son of Pæthµa, Arjuna; **¬≥ÿ** behold; **∞ÃÊŸ˜** these; **‚◊flÃÊŸ˜** assembled; **∑È§M§Ÿ˜** Kauravas; **ßÁÃÔ** thus. 

***[Bhagavad-G∂tµa ] *** 

**52** 

**Sa¤jaya said: O king, thus addressed by Arjuna, ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa placed the magnificent chariot between the two armies in front of Bh∂¶ma, Droƒa and all the kings and said, ëëArjuna, behold these Kauravas assembled here.íí (24-25)** 

The word ëGuŒµakµaí means ësleepí and a conqueror of sleep is called ëGuŒµake‹aí. Arjuna had conquered his sleep; that is, he could carry on lifeís activities without taking rest in the form of sleep, and never felt oppressed by sleep. He was never overcome by lethargy. By mentioning him as ëGuŒµake‹aí, Sa¤jayaís intention was to point out to Dhætarµa¶¢ra that he could never expect his sons to overcome Arjuna, who was always so alert and circumspect. 

By saying, ëbehold these Kauravas assembled hereí, Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa intimated that, in accordance with Arjunaís request that the chariot should be placed between the two armies and kept there till he had carefully observed all the warriors, He had brought the chariot between the two armies and had placed it at such a point that from there Arjuna could have a clear view of all the warriors. The chariot had been brought to a standstill, and Arjuna could observe the fighters as long as he liked. 

The word ëKauravasí in the sentence ëëbehold these Kauravasíí, has a special 

significance. By this, Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa intended to say that the fighters in that army were mostly members of Arjunaís own family, and his own kith and kin. It was an invitation to Arjuna to see those near and dear ones drawn up for battle. This covert suggestion from the Lord brought to light Arjunaís deep identification with, and attachment to his own family, which had up till now remained concealed within his heart. These words, as it were, acted as the seed from which sprung the faint-heartedness of Arjuna caused by compassion, the source of which was affection for blood relations. It appears that in order to make available His blessings to the world, using Arjuna as His instrument, the Lord through the use of these words produced such a dejection in Arjunaís mind that he refused to participate in the war, as the result of which there began to flow from the direct lips of God Himself the sweet and nectarean stream of words in the form of the divine _G∂tµa,_ which sanctified the three worlds, and uplifted, and will continue, for an infinite period of time, to uplift the souls of an infinite number of beings. 

_Hearing the above words of Bhagavµan ›r∂ Kæ¶ƒa what Arjuna did, is now being described by Sa¤jaya._
